macro_segments.title: Shmuel's Parameters for Basar be-Chalav and the Principle of Issur Chal al Issur

micro_segments.title: Shmuel's derivation of basar be-chalav rules from the words 'gedi' and 'imo'
The debate over whether issur chal al issur applies: does one prohibition take effect on another?
Three possible resolutions: general principle, special case for basar be-chalav, or conflicting amoraim positions

macro_segments.title: Parameters of Milk: Milk of an Animal Not Yet a Mother

micro_segments.title: The question of milk from a pregnant but not yet birthed female animal
The ruling that milk counts if the animal is female and will become a mother, excluding only male animals

macro_segments.title: Cooking Forbidden Fat or Meat with Milk: Independence or Dependency of Cooking and Eating Prohibitions

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's initial framing: do you get lashes for cooking fat and milk together?
Rabbi Ami and Rabbi Assi debate: whether cooking is independent (one opinion) or related to eating (other opinion)
Reframing the debate: cooking as primary concern vs. eating as primary concern
Two possible understandings: cooking and eating are independent, or eating is a derivative safeguard of cooking
Alternative view: cooking and eating related in reverse—if you get lashes for cooking, you get lashes for eating
Possible resolution: each opinion refers to a different act without actual disagreement

macro_segments.title: Exemptions from the Basar be-Chalav Prohibition in Cooking

micro_segments.title: Cooking with mei chalav (milk water/whey): exempt because it is not considered real milk
Cooking blood with milk: exempt because blood is not considered meat
Cooking bones, sinews, horns, and hooves with milk: exempt because they are not meat
Cooking piggul, nosar, and forbidden meat with milk: liable because the meat is already forbidden
The Gemara's analysis: the baraita assumes issur chal al issur and does not prove independence of cooking

macro_segments.title: Reish Lakish's Distinction: Mei Chalav for Ritual Impurity vs. Basar be-Chalav

micro_segments.title: The mishna's rule that mei chalav is like milk for making food susceptible to impurity
Reish Lakish's limitation: mei chalav counts only for ritual impurity purposes, not for basar be-chalav cooking
The baraita confirms mei chalav is not considered real milk for the basar be-chalav prohibition

macro_segments.title: Logical Expansion of Basar be-Chalav: From Gedi be-Chalav Imo to All Meat with All Milk

micro_segments.title: The tannaitic derivation: why basar be-chalav applies to other animals and species beyond goat and its mother
The kal va-chomer: mother is not forbidden to breed with offspring, yet cooking together is forbidden
First expansion: why the Torah must teach 'bachalav imo' to include cow's milk and sheep's milk

macro_segments.title: Need for Scriptural Verse: Excluding Close Cases and Including Distant Cases

micro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's objection: the kal va-chomer from crossbreeding seems sufficient without a verse
Counter-objection: mother-child have special severity in slaughter laws (shechitta), unlike crossbreeding
Therefore, the verse 'bachalav imo' is needed to teach the rules comprehensively

macro_segments.title: Expansion to Non-Mother Animals: The Sister's Milk Question

micro_segments.title: New question: cooking a kid in milk of its older sister, not its mother
Kal va-chomer argument: mother forbidden, older sister is different in ma'aser status, so forbidden too
Rav Ashi's objection: the kal va-chomer should suffice without another verse
Counter-objection: mother and child have special prohibitions in slaughter, unlike siblings
The common denominator emerges: the issue is basar be-chalav, not specific relationships

macro_segments.title: The Animal's Own Milk: Why Scripture is Needed to Include the Most Difficult Case

micro_segments.title: The question: cooking an animal in its own milk, when it is both mother to the milk and the milk-producer
First kal va-chomer: siblings cannot be slaughtered together, yet can be cooked together; mother and child cannot be slaughtered together, so milk and child should be forbidden
Objection via horse and mule: same mother can have children of different species; laws differ for forbidding vs. allowing relations by species of father
Second kal va-chomer attempt: the reverse case proves the issue is not about mother vs. sibling but about species identity
Third attempt: slave-woman analogy to show the principle is about status (slave vs. free), not family relationship
Fourth attempt: kilai zera'im (crossbreeding seeds)—but even here the mother (earth) is necessarily involved
Final explanation by Rav Ashi: when it is one entity (animal with its own milk), only one body is involved, unlike two separate animals (mother and offspring)
Conclusion: therefore the verse 'bachalav imo' was necessary to include this case of an animal with its own milk

topical_tags.term: issur chal al issur (one prohibition takes effect on another)
piggul (sacrificial offering invalidated by wrong intention)
kal va-chomer (logical inference from light to heavy)
ta'ar ha-din (the shape/form of the law returns and goes back)
